title: Woven table runner on a rigid heddle loom
subtitle: A striped linen-cotton runner in tabby weave with a hemstitched edge
excerpt: Set up a full-width warp in a striped linen-cotton yarn, weave a plain tabby table runner, and finish the ends with hemstitch. The finished piece is about 35 cm wide and 120 cm long, depending on loom width.
sourceNotes: Tabby table runner weaving is a classic beginner-to-intermediate rigid-heddle project in UK weaving guilds. Hemstitch finishing is described in standard weaving manuals.

glossaryTerms.definition: The threads stretched lengthways on the loom before weaving. The warp determines the width and length of the finished runner.
The number of warp threads per centimetre or inch. A 7.5 dpi reed suits 8/2 cotton; a 10 dpi reed suits 10/2 or 12/2 cotton for a crisper hand.
A decorative finishing stitch worked on the loom at the beginning and end of a woven piece. It groups warp threads in small bundles and secures the weft edge without fringing.
Plain weave: the weft passes alternately over and under each warp thread. All rigid-heddle weaving in standard up/down position is tabby.

content.text: A table runner is a practical, visible project that shows off the

stripe pattern well and gives the weaver practice in maintaining consistent selvedge edges over a longer piece. Linen-cotton or 100% linen warp yarn gives a crisper finish than pure cotton for table use and washes well. The
weave is straightforward, but maintaining even beat over a metre of weaving takes practice.

items.name: 8/2 cotton or linen-cotton warp yarn, 2 or 3 colours
Rigid-heddle loom, minimum 40 cm weaving width

content.text: Calculate the warp using the desired finished width plus 10 percent take-up allowance. Set the

at 7.5 or 10 dpi to match the yarn weight. Warp length: 160 cm for a 120 cm finished runner, plus 40 cm loom waste. Warp in your chosen stripe sequence, threading each end through a hole or slot in the heddle with the reed hook.

after the first two rows of weaving while the warp edge is still accessible. Group warp threads in pairs; bring the needle up between each pair from back to front, then run it under the pair from right to left, and loop around the pair from front to back. Weave the body of the runner in plain tabby, changing weft colour every 10 to 20 rows for horizontal stripes if desired. Beat each row with the same pressure; use the hemstitch as a reference for even beat by measuring the rows per centimetre periodically. Finish with a second hemstitch at the far end before cutting off.

Cut from the loom and trim warp tails to 3 cm where not hemstitched. Machine wash at 40°C to full the linen slightly and set the weave. Press flat with an iron while still slightly damp. The first wash may shrink the piece a further 5 to 8 percent; this is expected and desirable for a table runner.

items.problem: The runner narrows to a waist at the centre
items.cause: Weft tension increased gradually as weaving progressed
items.fix: Arc the weft more generously before beating on every row. Check the width with a ruler every 20 rows.

items.problem: The hemstitch groups are uneven in size
items.cause: Counting errors in the number of threads per group
items.fix: Mark every sixth warp thread with a small piece of waste yarn before hemstitching. Work consistently into groups of 2 or 3 threads only.
